title: Neural Training
do_not_use_for: generic CPU task systems without neural intent
rules: separate forward, loss, backward, accumulation, optimizer, checkpoint, and telemetry phases
keep parameter, gradient, accumulator, and optimizer-state ownership clear
do not mix training orchestration with unrelated runtime systems
treat diagnostic readback as optional and bounded
procedure: Define dataset samples, batch layout, inputs, targets, and masks.
Map batch buffers into runtime tensor contracts.
Compute loss and write output gradients.
Clear or preserve state according to the accumulation policy.
Record telemetry and checkpoint only through explicit boundaries.

primary_when: request is about neural training loops, backpropagation, optimizer application, batch processing, loss, gradients, checkpoints, or training telemetry
request is about updating model weights from loss and gradients
must_not_own_when: request is mainly about UI rendering, runtime patching, or non-neural CPU task systems

solves: how to run training through explicit tensor ownership instead of hidden framework state
how to keep batch, target, loss, gradient, optimizer, and telemetry phases reviewable
how to prevent diagnostics and checkpointing from becoming hidden training bottlenecks
does_not_solve: generic job scheduling outside neural training

core_principles: Training is a sequence of tensor state transitions.
Parameters, gradients, accumulators, optimizer moments, activations, targets, and masks must be distinct tensor roles.
Batch shape is part of the runtime contract, not just loop metadata.
Loss computation owns both scalar reporting and output-gradient production.
Optimizer state belongs to the same runtime ownership model as parameters.
Telemetry should explain cost without forcing synchronization every batch.
constraints: Do not allocate new hot-path tensors per batch when arena reuse is possible.
Do not download gradients for logging by default in performance-sensitive runs.
Do not clear accumulators, optimizer state, or activations implicitly without a phase rule.
Do not mix target masks and prediction tensors without an explicit shape contract.
Do not save checkpoints from stale CPU mirrors if GPU-resident state is the source of truth.
decision_rules: Use training as primary when the request mentions backprop, loss, optimizer, gradient, batch, epoch, checkpoint, or training telemetry.
Attach tensor-runtime-architecture for every training task because training correctness depends on tensor roles and ownership.
Attach optimization when the issue involves throughput, stability, gradient norm, precision, or transfer cost.
Attach architecture when layer forward/backward contracts need to change.
Use CPU fallback as a correctness reference only when the runtime boundary is explicit.

preferred_patterns: phase-separated training loop: apply inputs, forward, loss, backward, accumulate, optimize, clear, telemetry
batch provider that owns CPU staging but not runtime tensor lifetime
loss functions that write gradients into explicit gradient tensors
optimizer state tensors allocated in the arena
bounded telemetry frames with arena usage, phase timings, token metrics, and optional gradient stats
avoid_patterns: loss functions returning only scalars while hiding gradient writes
optimizer moments stored in unrelated arrays
gradient monitoring that downloads all parameter gradients every batch by default
batch-size changes that leak old tensor slices
checkpoint code that ignores tensor residency state

recipes.name: Design a training phase contract
recipes.when: Use when adding or reviewing a custom training loop.
steps: List every phase and the tensor roles it reads or writes.
Define input, target, mask, prediction, loss-gradient, parameter-gradient, accumulator, and optimizer-state shapes.
Define which tensors persist across batches and which are scratch.
Define when gradients are cleared, accumulated, clipped, or logged.
Define telemetry fields and ensure they do not force hidden full-state readback.

recipes.name: Add a new optimizer
recipes.when: Use when implementing SGD, Adam, or another update rule in the custom runtime.
steps: Define parameter and gradient tensor inputs.
Allocate optimizer state tensors through the arena.
Represent the update as command-stream or backend-executable operations.
Keep weight decay, momentum, bias correction, epsilon, and precision behavior explicit.
Add a deterministic correctness comparison on a small tensor.

recipes.name: Checkpoint runtime-owned training state
recipes.when: Use when saving or restoring parameters from a GPU-resident or hybrid runtime.
steps: Identify the source of truth for parameters and optimizer state.
Schedule explicit download or staging only for tensors required by the checkpoint.
Record shape, precision, optimizer metadata, backend label, and dataset/training context.
Avoid using stale CPU mirrors unless the mirror is marked current.
Validate restored tensors against expected runtime shapes before training resumes.

pitfalls: Assuming batch buffers and runtime tensors share ownership.
Letting loss metrics trigger prediction downloads in the hot path.
Using gradient clipping that silently mutates downloaded CPU copies without syncing back.
Clearing accumulators before optimizer commands consume them.
Treating checkpoint serialization as a harmless read when tensors are GPU-resident.
review_checklist: Are parameter, gradient, accumulator, optimizer-state, activation, input, target, and mask tensors separate?
Does loss computation write gradients through a documented tensor contract?
Are gradient logging and clipping bounded and synchronized deliberately?
Does the optimizer operate on runtime-owned tensors?
Are batch-size changes and arena reservations handled?
Can checkpointing identify the current source of truth?

references.title: Neural Training Runtime
references.interpretation: Use this first for phase contracts, tensor roles, gradients, optimizers, telemetry, and checkpoints.
references.why: Training is the domain's primary stress test for tensor ownership.

references.title: Neural Tensor Runtime Architecture
references.interpretation: Use for tensor layout and arena ownership behind training phases.
references.why: Training correctness depends on the tensor runtime.

references.title: Neural GPU Residency
references.interpretation: Use when training state is GPU-resident or hybrid.
references.why: Checkpoints, telemetry, and gradients must respect the data source of truth.
